speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other jobs than handing the boxes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. And, again, I don't-- that one I don't remember. But a man named Adler wrote a number of books on Theresienstadt because he was there. And, again, my father knew this man and sent him some of our papers. And lo and behold, in his book appears a call. A paper that concerns me. And it's a call up to come at 7 o'clock in the morning in November, mind you, to a certain building, with the other children, of course, to go and gather chestnuts. Now, I have no recall of any trees in Theresienstadt, number one, leave alone chestnut trees. These were horse chestnuts, and I suppose they were used for the horses. The Nazis did have horses. And they rode their horses through the town. My father, to begin with, was a street cleaner-- the old fashioned kind, with a cart and a shovel and a broom made of twigs. And I can see him to the day of today cleaning up horse manure. Where there's horse manure, there are horses. But I don't remember that I picked up these horse chestnuts, nor do I remember what we put them in, or how we got them, or anything else about it. But if there is an official call up paper, then I went to gather horse chestnuts, for sure. And that was in November '44, so just after, and possibly still during, the fall transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the art studios?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. What I know about it I have read afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't remember, other than Yos Speer, whom I remember as the father of one of the people I knew. And I knew that he was an artist. I also know that for one of my birthdays my father asked him to make a little drawing for a necklace, that my father's pharmacy assistant, who in prewar life was a goldsmith, then made-- sawed out of brass. And it showed a little girl, running, with an outstretched arm, in which was a letter. And it had my name on the bottom and 1944 on top. It was for my 11th birthday. And Yos Speer drew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't have that anymore, do you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have the original. I had another one made because I know exactly what it looked like.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family stay together the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Together in Theresienstadt and Westerbork, yes. Together in the same room? No. Or in the same barracks? No. In Westerbork, we were in the same barracks, but on opposite sides. And my father in one big room, and we in the other. In Theresienstadt, we were moved around an awful lot. And in the beginning, we were in the same barracks-- the Hamburg barracks-- where all Dutch Jews went. My father in one room, and we-- my mother and I-- in another. Later, for some, reason my father, for a while, was in the [NON-ENGLISH] barracks. Then we went there, and my father went elsewhere. Then we all ended up in a small room, together with my friend Hans and his mother-- five of us-- in a room that was meant for one. And then towards the end, when the Russians liberated us, my parents and I lived together in one room in one of what used to be private houses in a street called Lake Street, which had no lake, and was probably called something else before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Hans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hans was the only friend I had in Theresienstadt. I talked to a number of children, both in Westerbork and Theresienstadt. We played together, we talked together, but we weren't friends, as such. Hans was a friend. He was a couple of years younger than I, but more or less from the day we met, we were inseparable. We both went where the other went. Whatever it is we did, be that to go for food, or see whether we could steal some fuel or-- or food, or something else, or whether we were just walking around, or whatever, but we were always together. And since he was younger, he was, let's see, eight to my 10, so he stayed with his mother in the women's room. So we were together even there. And we played together, in as far as any of us children really played. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Games were camp games. In the morning, we had an every morning ritual, which became a game, but was really a necessity, and that was to see who could catch more fleas and bed bugs and kill more. Since we were infested with them-- I earlier mentioned the wooden beds-- the bed bugs crawl into the wood, and eat their way in, and as a result, even when we were chased out of the barracks, and the barracks were then poison gassed to get rid of the vermin, they simply went deeper into the wood and survived it very nicely. So we came back to the same vermin, needles to say. They are easier to catch than fleas because they don't jump.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it takes a certain amount of training, however, because, of course, nobody was used to that, even the poorest of the poor pre-war did not have vermin in their houses like that. So nobody was used to it. But Hans and I were children. We had by then adapted to that kind of life. I can't call it anything else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was just an every day thing that needed to be done, and so we did it, and so we made a game of it. It's that simple. We decided what to catch, and then at a certain moment, we would catch for, let's say, five or 10 minutes, and then we would count the deads-- the dead ones. And whoever had more had won, and that was that. Typical camp game. Another camp game was we would go up to the attics. The barracks were meant for soldiers, of course, so the attics really didn't have rooms, except in the four corners. And often they had been used for storage, and now they were also used for people. In between rooms, there was nothing. There were open beams, and you could kind of look through on whatever it was they used for ceilings below. And in those open spaces lived rats. I don't know how they lived, they had no more to eat than we did, but they lived. And one of our games was to poke them with a stick and make them jump, preferably over the beam. And we thought that was very funny at the time. They never-- they did jump. They never attacked us, for whatever reason, either because they didn't have strength, or who knows why, but they didn't. And in those attics, presumably for moving things up and down, they had arches. And they would have probably hauled things up on a pulley and moved them in. There were no doors in those arches. Probably none were needed originally. And Hans and I would stand in there. And standing on what was essentially the fourth floor, you could see out over the ghetto walls. Since it was a 45 city, it had, of course, the barbed wire, but it also had heavy walls like Quebec does, on which grew grass. But they weren't all that high, and so standing on the fourth floor, you could see out over them. And you didn't really see much, it was open country. But it gave us the idea that there was something out there that was not limited space, limited privacy, too many people, too much noise, too much dust, too much dirt, not enough food. And so we had all sorts of fantasy games in which we would fly away to wherever we wanted, preferably home, of course. Because we didn't really know what home was anymore by that time. Things-- too many things had happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's easy to talk about going home, but what is home? Did that mean being with your parents, or did that mean being all in one room, or all in one apartment? We talked about going to school, too, but we really didn't know anymore what that was. What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last bit of schooling we had was, as I earlier described, with a lot of teacher turnover, a lot of student turnover. We didn't know what a real, honest to goodness, regular school was like. We also didn't know that a regular school doesn't teach you to lie, naturally. So we talked about it, but we didn't really know what we were saying. But we played. We played with my doll, also. Both of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No he didn't. He and his brother and his parents went on those fall transports, in fall of '44, and then we talked about their being transported east, or transported to Poland. Everything was east in Poland, even though it really wasn't, necessarily. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for the longest time, for the next 40 years, or so, I did not know what had happened to him. And then, in one of my first letters to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], I asked them that they have my family and me, and Hans' family and him, in their cards. And they wrote back and said, yes, they had us. And by the way, that family was with such and such transport, on such and such a date, transported to Auschwitz and gassed on arrival. And that was just like a-- like somebody had hit me over the head with a stone block. Because it came totally unexpectedly. I had never thought that I would ever find out what happened to him. And it's hard to believe even now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we all have, I think, fantasies, when you don't know what happened to somebody, that maybe you'll meet them. I still think that about Hans, even though I know up there with my brain that no, can't be, because he's dead. But he isn't really dead, not to me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So then that was in October '44, as I now know. And after that, I was mostly by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anything that terrified you? Any violence, or was it all-- were they all taken away to a violent death, but was there was peace in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no to both. No, I don't remember seeing anything, either because I actually didn't see it, or because it's all gone blank. I don't know which. But, yes, violence was done. When the Nazis came by, as they very often did, through the streets, if you didn't go out of the way soon enough, or you didn't stand at attention well enough, or whatever, at least for the adult, they were just as likely as not to either slap you or whip you, or whatever. I do not remember seeing it. In the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] painting studio that you mentioned earlier, there were several of the painters that were taken and tortured not in Theresienstadt itself, but in the second fortress, what was called the Little Fortress, which was used as a prison within a prison. And if you had committed any kind of a crime-- be that stealing a piece of bread or a cigarette, or whatever-- we weren't supposed to have any cigarettes, needless to say. Or in the painter's case, painting the real Theresienstadt, instead of what they were told to paint, they were sent to the Little Fortress and either tortured to death or sent on. And one of the people to whom that happened is an artist who went by the name of Fritta. Friedrich Taschlisch, or Taschlisch was his name. Sometimes, instead of Friedrich, he's called Bedrich. His son survived. He was then about three, I think, and he was adopted by one of the other artists, Leo Haas, and they both live in Israel now. But his-- the father, Fritta himself, did not survive. And that was-- he went to the Little Fortress, he and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you seen the exhibit by the [? Lexi? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I've seen it. I've seen the exhibit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it hangs now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Martyr's Memorial, as far as I know. That's where I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's unusual about that exhibit?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was unusual to me is that he-- Leschly had managed to paint the real Theresienstadt, cut up the paintings, and hide them in such a way that they were not found until after the war. And that, incidentally, is the real Theresienstadt as I know it, because you can see-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there's one painting showing people-- children and adults-- gathering around, and grubbing through a sort of a trash heap. That's exactly what Hans and I did for food. There is one showing the wooden bunk beds. There is-- there are several, I think, showing the lines for food. So that's the Theresienstadt I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leschly?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, lives in Israel, too, today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went back to find the cut up pieces. How did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure. Either he went back to find the cut up pieces and put them together, or repaint them, or somebody found them.

speaker: interviewer
text: He put them in a jar I believe, or jars or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know. He must have put them in something to conserve them, but I don't know how he hid them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he re-painted them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he  did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I believe that the exhibit has the real ones that were cut up, as well as the repainted ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and they hang together. You can see the whole thing in the repainting. And then, like a puzzle-- like puzzle pieces almost, next to it or underneath it. So, it's very interesting. But it's definitely the real Theresienstadt. So, adults would remember more than I, but even I recognize it as the real Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of the people that you came in with to Theresienstadt do you think survived, and how many were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. The transport with which I came-- my parents and I-- numbered about 870 people or so. How many of those survived, I don't know. Partly because I don't know whether they stayed in Theresienstadt, as we did, or whether they were further transported to, let's say, Auschwitz, or any of those camps, where they had far fewer chance-- far less chance of surviving. There are very few children, however, that survived. Out of the roughly 15,000 children below 15 that went through Theresienstadt, either stayed or went through to Auschwitz, and so on, the survival rate is roughly 125. They used to say 100, but a friend of mine in New York, Inga Auerbacher, and I have put together a directory of Theresienstadt children, and between us, we already have over 100 names, so obviously it's more, but it's not more than about 125, 150 at the very most. Out of 15,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of 15,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: So. Now, over 15, that's something else. They had a better chance, for one thing, if they stayed in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you liberated, and by whom? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very well, because I saw them come in. Liberated by the Russian army on May 8, '45. And by that time, my parents and I were living in what I said earlier was called Lake Street [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And right next to that ran a railroad spur. Originally, the railroad station was outside of Theresienstadt, but at a spur had been built already before we came. And, of course, there was a fence. And I remember standing near that wooden fence and watching the soldiers come in. Not really marching in, but coming in groups of two, or three, or five. Kind of, you know, dribbling in. And last December, so it was December '94, I was talking to Inga on the phone, Inga Auerbacher. And she was all excited because she had found somebody in New York that had been, as she was, on that fence, watching those soldiers come in. And I said, I was there, too. And that is the first time that that has been confirmed for me that that was really so. Nobody but nobody knew anything about it or would talk about it. It had never been confirmed. And it wasn't until last December. So I'm very pleased about that. So I remember it very well. Then they made the Nazis do the work that we had done before. They shaved, not their whole head, but a stripe in the middle. And on the back of their uniform jacket, they painted a great big enormous red S for, again, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and they had to do all the street cleaning and work and so on that we did. We didn't-- in that sense, we were liberated. We were not liberated in the sense that we didn't get more food, because the Russians didn't have anything either. And we couldn't leave right away, for various reasons. A, there was a typhus epidemic. So typhus spreads very quickly, anyway. There's no way they were going to let us out. B, there was no transportation to be had. So when, finally, the day came that the first ones could leave-- the Czechs were the first to go, because they were in their own country. I have no idea whether they walked home, or how they got home, but at least they spoke the language. We didn't. As far as my parents and I were concerned, we stayed-- we had to stay-- until about June 15 or so. I wrote down June 20 on that questionnaire, that's not quite true. I think it was about June 15. Then, somehow, we got to Pilsen, well known for its beer, and there we were quartered for a week in what turned out to be the local insane asylum. Sorry, but that's what it was. That's what they were called at the time. Where the mentally ill were pushed out of their rooms, essentially, into one half of the building, and we got the other half of the building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very nice, clean sheets, real beds, lovely, with the occasional food in between meals. I don't remember any meals. But I remember getting-- every afternoon-- a piece of bread with probably margarine on it and some sugar. I took that piece of bread, and there was a tree with very low branches. And I climbed that tree and ate my bread in peace and quiet, because nobody knew I was up there, so nobody could steal it. And once I'd eaten it, of course, it was safe. We stayed there for about a week. We were allowed to go into town. We didn't all that much, partly because we were probably too weak, and partly because the inhabitants of the town did give us rather odd looks. And then after that week was over, again, somehow, we went to a field-- literally a field-- a meadow. And everybody stood there looking at the sky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were some other children. There was a Professor Myers, a friend of my father's, a Dutchman, and he had his daughter with him. And two of us were looking at each other, kind of going, what is all this? You know, looking at the sky. There wasn't anything there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it turned out that a plane came. We had never seen a plane, of course. That turned out to be an army transport plane. And that took us back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, a number of the Dutch had already left. How, I don't know. This was the last group, and my parents and I went back to Holland, and we were flown to Eindhoven, famous for the Philips Electronics factories, then and now. And it was, in fact, in those factories that we were quartered, because they were empty at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once again, a big room, no beds, good mattresses though, but on the floor. Probably couldn't come up with enough beds for all of us. And the very first thing that happens-- happened was we went in there, and we were assigned a mattress. They were bright red. I don't know why, but they were. And a men tapped me on the shoulder and said-- in Dutch, till then, we had spoken German-- so this was a big surprise. And he said, here, I've got something for you. And he gave me something. And it was a piece, maybe about yea big, of something brown.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just looked at him. I had no idea what to do with this thing. And he looked at me full of expectation, and I didn't know what to do with it. And he finally said, well, aren't you going to eat it? And all I could think of was that the soap in Theresienstadt had been that exact same color and size. And I said, I can't eat soap. I thought, my God, here I'm home, what's happening now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother had a real hard time to persuade me that this was honest to goodness chocolate, and it was safe to eat. And it took me a long time before I tried it. I wouldn't be surprised if she had to eat a piece of it before I would try. But that's what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12. But those things continued. We stayed there for a week. And then by truck, we were trucked back to Amsterdam. And people were left off this truck along the way, wherever they wanted to go. But we went to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: But five years after the war-- I was 17 by then-- my mother and I were walking past the Greengrocer's shop. They were all over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw something that looked to me very strange. And I said, mommy, what's that? Pointing, of course. Kind of pointing, because I'd been taught you don't point.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother took a look and, of course, laughed in my face. She said, you're a typical war child. That's a banana. I had never seen a banana in my whole life. I-- didn't tell me very much, obviously. There was a lady passing by just then, a woman. And she looked at my mother, and looked at me. And you could just tell what she was thinking. She thought I was retarded. And these things kept happening. They still happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somewhere along the line, and it probably goes, if not for all of us, for many of us, nobody seems to have had time after the war to really have taught us the relatively minor things that go with socialization. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as a result, when something happens, I don't know what to do. For example, I don't know how to start a conversation, because I was never taught. And something that happened in my synagogue not too long ago, somebody got very angry at me because that person said that I made that person very uncomfortable because I was always thanking her for things that she did. And it was too much, and it was excessive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was most upset, because I had no idea. It took me a week to figure out not only that yes, you can do that too much, but why didn't I know? And it took me a week to figure out I didn't know because nobody ever told me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I call them gaps. And I think we probably all have them. Because it was-- it was much more important to teach us not to lie anymore and not to steal anymore, and, yes, this is a banana, and no, you can't take it. The rest kind of went by the wayside. The adults also had to adjust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to the lying and the cheating that you needed to unlearn? How soon did that leave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably within the year. I don't know that my-- either of my parents took me apart and said, now, you've done this, and this, and this, and now that's no good anymore. But when something came up, I think that's when I got a lecture. Let's just say that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know-- I said I hadn't done something that I had, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you get an invasive question, your reaction no longer is to lie first.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At this point, I can honestly say, I don't lie. I would rather say nothing than to say an untruth. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Equally, I don't cheat, and I don't steal. That was easier to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you back to school right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not right away because we came home in June, and that's summer vacation. Dutch elementary schools, then and possibly now, started in August. So in August, I went back to school. And, again, there were problems there because when we left, when we were deported, I was in second grade-- had pretty well finished second grade. But third and fourth never happened. So now I was stuck in fifth grade, which was more age appropriate, but I hadn't the foggiest idea what had happened in the last two years. And for that matter, to a great extent, I still don't know. I haven't needed what I don't know. And I can look up what I don't know, but the fact remains, I never learned it. And that fifth grade went from, oh, about 11-year-olds all the way up to 16-year-olds, because people had been in camps, they had been in hiding, they had been in Germany and work camps, various other reasons. So everybody was in a mess. And that situation lasted all the way through high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America, and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1959. February 4, 1959. How? Well, I came, actually, on what was supposed to be a visit. I had had a job in Holland as a translator/proofreader for a medical journal, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and quit the job, went to England, where by then my parents lived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't find a job there, even if only because I didn't have the nationality. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So between my parents and me, we decided maybe I should go to secretarial school. I am profoundly grateful I never did. But anyway, that's what we decided. And that didn't start right away, so my parents decided to send me to the United States for three or four months to friends of ours to see how life was lived here. And that's what we did, except then I decided I wanted to stay. And the one and only time that my German birth has made something good happen to me was then, because it was still a quota system. The Dutch quota was pretty well full. The German quota was wide open. And I didn't even have to go home, or go to Canada, or whatever to get my first papers. I could stay right were I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your parents come with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
